data.title: Snowflake: Agentic AI And Data Cloud Winner - Upgrade To Speculative Buy
data.description:
I previously reiterated my Hold rating for Snowflake (NYSE:SNOW) in September 2025, given the risks from the overstretched valuations/technical indicators notably negating its renewed SaaS growth prospects.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am finally upgrading the SNOW stock as a Speculative Buy after numerous Hold/Sell ratings, given the improved risk/reward after the recent SaaS meltdown and the emergence of a technical bottoming.

This is especially since the AI SaaS company has continued to prove its relevance through accretive M&A activities and in-house R&D efforts, all of which underscoring their relevance during the ongoing Agentic AI and Data Cloud boom.

SNOW's Agentic AI & Data Cloud Story Looks More Convincing Here

Since then, my caution surrounding SNOW's potentially painful correction has indeed materialized as previously posited, with the stock experiencing a steep SaaSpocalypse pullback along the wider SaaS sector.

This development lent credence to my prior warning surrounding the risks of chasing overpriced stocks at a time of AI exuberance, since it was apparent that its valuations had ran away from its fundamentals then.

Otherwise, there is no denying SNOW's robust AI-driven growth prospects, with the integration of their core AI Data Cloud offering across their new capabilities already streamlining their customers' data life cycle across acquisition, analysis, interpretation, and production while delivering accelerated productivity, including:

  • Snowflake Intelligence (Generative/Agentic AI analytical capabilities),
  • Cortex Code (AI coding agent for agent/app building and productivity workflows), and
  • strategic model partnership with numerous LLM leaders, including Google (GOOG), OpenAI, and Anthropic.

Without regurgitating SNOW's FQ4'26 numbers, it is apparent that the higher "telemetry activity on the platform" (automated data monitoring/analysis) have triggered the customers' increased consumption trends and the consequently growing product revenues - all of which underscoring their Agentic AI beneficiary status.

Readers must not forget the tailwinds from their excellent go-to-market strategy and the focus on larger Enterprise deals on their performance metrics, with it already contributing to the outsized multi-year Remaining Performance Obligation's [RPO] expansion to $9.77B in FQ4'26 (+23.9% QoQ/+42.4% YoY).

This is on top of the tailwinds from SNOW's recent acquisitions:

  • Observe - allowing them to offer improved data monitoring outcome and operational resilience,
  • Datometry Migration Solution - allowing them to migrate legacy data to SNOW's data warehouse at "lower cost and with minimal disruption" and naturally, reduce friction/acquire new customers easily, along with
  • Select Star - allowing them to expand their in-house data catalog through Select Star's embedded metadata and data discovery capability,

with it underscoring their focus on delivering renewed growth and expanded data capabilities while effectively putting their prior FQ4'24 revenue growth headwinds (arising from the introduction of the tiered storage pricing and the increased adoption of the open-source Iceberg Table formats) behind them.

These reasons are also why SNOW has been able to report robust adoption/cross selling trends and the rich Net Revenue Retention Rate of 125% (-1 points YoY).

This is while defying the recent sector pessimism surrounding the SaaS monetization trends, as observed in:

  • the higher net add for the Customers Over $1M Product Revenue at 157 (compared to a year ago at 125) and
  • the total customers at 2.33K (compared to a year ago 1.18K),

significantly aided by their consumption-based pricing model against that of the per-seat model.

This is especially since SNOW has walked the talk on AI delivering improved productivities, as they hint at an improved framework "for investing in growth. It's no longer tied to headcount," with it implying their ability to drive further operating leverage/efficiencies moving forward.

The same has been observed in their expanding adj operating income to $139.17M in FQ4'26 (+49.9% YoY) and adj operating margins to 11% (+2 points YoY), with FY2027 likely to bring forth richer bottom-line performance if the management sustains their current framework.

These reasons may also be why SNOW has felt confident to offer a promising FY2027 guidance across:

  • revenue growth to $5.66B (+26.6% YoY),
  • adj operating margins of 12.5% (+2.5 points YoY), and
  • adj Free Cash Flow margins of 23% (-2 points YoY),

with it building upon the robust FY2026 performance at +29.1% YoY, 10% (+4 points YoY), and 25% (-1 points YoY), respectively.

The consensus have notably raised their forward estimates, with SNOW expected to report an excellent top-line growth at a CAGR of +24.5% through FY2029, with the acceleration against 'the expanding AI Data Cloud Total Addressable Market at 5Y CAGR of +15.8% to $335B in CY2029' implying their tailwinds from the ongoing Agentic AI boom.

The same has been observed in the projected acceleration in their adj EPS at a CAGR of +39.4% through FY2029 and the management's projection of SBC expenses to decline from FY2025 revenue ratio of 41% to FY2027 ratio of ~27%, with it lending credibility to their slower headcount growth and AI-driven operating efficiencies over the next few years.

Focus On EV/Sales, Pending Bottom-Line Acceleration

On the one hand, despite the raised consensus forward estimates and the -46.2% stock price correction from the 52 week highs, it is apparent that SNOW remains expensive at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 84.21x, despite the notable moderation from the 4Y mean of 324.87x (the period of time that they are adj EPS profitable).

A similar conclusion may also be derived against my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.13x, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 84.21x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +39.4%, compared to the 4Y mean of 1.52x and the sector median of 1.27x.

On the other hand, part of SNOW's premium valuations may be attributed to its FY2026 Rule of 54% outperformance (-2 points YoY), based on the FY2026 revenue growth by +29% YoY (compared to a year ago at +30% YoY) and FY2026 FCF margins of 25% (compared to a year ago at 26%).

When comparing SNOW's FWD EV/Sales valuations of 8.46x and the FY2026 Rule of 54% outperformance to its diversified AI SaaS peers, including:

  • Enterprise/hyperscaler - Oracle (ORCL) at 7.86x/-41.1% (LTM revenue growth by +14.9% YoY and FCF margins of -56%),
  • Enterprise/hyperscaler - Microsoft (MSFT) at 8.23x/42% (LTM revenue growth by +16.7% sequentially and FCF margins of 25.3%),
  • Customer Relationship Management - Salesforce (CRM) at 3.91x/44% (FY2026 revenue growth by +10% YoY and FCF margins of 34%), and
  • Enterprise/Federal analytics focus - Palantir (PLTR) at 44.33x/107% (FY2025 revenue growth by +56% YoY and FCF margins of 51%), respectively,

it is apparent that SNOW profitable growth trends (on a revenue and FCF basis) has been well regarded by the market, with it contributing to the still premium stock price valuations despite the recent selloff.

This is pending the multi-year improvement in its operating leverage on an adj operating margin and/or adj EPS basis.

If anything, SNOW has been putting much of their FCF generation to good use, with the $873M of shares repurchased in FY2026 (-54.9% YoY from a year ago at $1.93B) balancing the dilutive impact of its elevated SBC expenses, as observed in the somewhat reasonable shares outstanding expansion to 342.29M (+3.2% YoY).

Combined with the still healthy balance sheet at a net cash position of $1.75B (-25.5% YoY), I am of the opinion that the management has been highly competent in their capital allocation across M&A growth opportunities and shareholder return thus far.

SNOW Is Cautiously Upgraded As A Speculative Buy

For now, SNOW has already returned much of their 2025 gains, with it remaining to be seen if the bulls may defend the $150s after bouncing off those levels twice in February 2026.

I am of the opinion that the worst of SNOW's correction may already be over, aided by the oversold indicators across the RSIs/trading volumes.

This is especially since the management's FY2027 revenue growth outlook by +27% YoY (compared to a year ago at +29% YoY) and FY2027 FCF margin outlook of 23% (-2 points YoY) imply at a robust FY2027 Rule of 50% performance (-4 points YoY), with further expansion likely based on the three beat and raise performances between FQ1'26-FQ3'26.

Combined with the potential bottoming at current levels, I am cautiously upgrading the SNOW stock as a Speculative Buy here.

This is albeit with the caveat that the AI SaaS sector is likely to underperform in the near term, attributed to the rapidly developing macroeconomic/geopolitical environment triggering the pessimistic stock market sentiments and the uncertain market rotation from the current emphasis on value stocks to Big Tech/AI/growth stocks.

This implies that SNOW may very well continue consolidating at these levels pending that recovery catalyst, with the stock only suitable for those with a longer investing trajectory.

data.title: Massive March Jobs Beat: Stock Futures And Bonds Trade Lower On Good Friday
data.description:
The March jobs report was much better than expected. The 178,000 payrolls rise was above even the highest economist’s estimate and was the best gain since December 2024. Of course, there may have been some positive payback from weak early-year labor market trends driven by one-off factors.

The three-month average number of jobs created is now 68,000 (above the perceived breakeven level required to keep the unemployment rate steady). Just 7,000 jobs were taken off from January and February, softer than the trend from the past eight months.

The unemployment rate fell sharply from 4.441% in February to 4.256% in March. The U-6 “underemployment” rate, however, rose to 8.0%. Average hourly earnings were particularly weak, up just 0.2% in March from February, bringing the year-over-year rate to just 3.5%--the lowest since May 2021. Average hours worked slipped to 34.2 per week from 34.3.

Elsewhere, manufacturing employment rose by 15,000, the most since November 2023, and the Health Care sector was responsible for 76,000 of the headline NFP gain. What's more, the government shed 8,000 positions last month. The number of permanent job losers fell to 1.9 million, while the percentage of unemployed workers for more than 27 weeks rose to 25.4% from 25.3% in February. The prime age labor force participation rate inched lower to 83.8% from 83.9%.

In markets, equity futures took their cue from the bond market. Treasury yields rose in electronic trading, putting downward pressure on S&P 500 futures (which closed at 9:15 a.m. ET for Good Friday).

March Unemployment Rate Falls, Big Headline Jobs Gain

Massive beat +178k March jobs... best since December 2024

The economy added an average of 15,000 jobs over the past six months and 68,000 per month so far in 2026

+186k private payrolls... best since December 2024

Very cool: Average hourly earnings just +3.5% YoY... lowest since May 2021

+15k Manufacturing payrolls.. best since November 2023

Fresh multi-month low in the unemployment rate.. 4.256%.. almost rounded down to 4.2%

The unemployment rate ticked down to 4.3%, but the U-6 underemployment rate rose to 8.0%

Equity Futures Fell Following the NFP Data

Treasury Yields Rose After the March Jobs Report

U.S. Recession Risk Has Dropped to 38%

Looking ahead, more key March data hits the tape on Monday with the ISM Services PMI data—be on watch for the key Prices Paid subindex. Durable Goods, a February data point, prints Tuesday morning, followed by an early-year look at Consumer Credit. More market-moving updates begin on Wednesday, highlighted by the minutes from the March FOMC meeting.

Jobless claims and a third look at Q4 GDP keep the macro busy, but Friday’s March CPI report will be the real volatility driver. Finally, a preliminary read on UMich Consumer Sentiment rounds out the week, with bank earnings the following week.

FOMC Minutes, CPI Next Week

The Bottom Line

It was a very strong March jobs report. The 178,000 payrolls gain was well above the 51,000 consensus, while the unemployment rate fell sharply to 4.26%. The market response was weak, as the data leave the Fed in a tough spot with elevated inflation.

data.title: Wheaton Precious Metals: BHP Deal Adds Even More Risk At Elevated Prices
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered Wheaton Precious Metals Corp. (WPM), I highlighted their solid but relatively risky exposure to gold and silver, with a leading growth pipeline, strong financials, and a good track record, rating them a Hold since their stock was trading around a fair value for the commodity prices we saw back then.

With the stock up as a result of their strong performance tied to the jump in gold and silver prices, WPM remains a Hold, trading slightly above what I would consider a fair value (same as before), while the company’s overall quality makes it worth paying attention to despite the geographic exposure and broader macro risks.

Foundation Still Shines

WPM’s 2025 was solid overall, with sales nearly doubling from $1.28 billion in 2024 to $2.31 billion in 2025 and net earnings nearly tripling to $1.47 billion, further highlighting the kind of leverage this type of company offers, beating their gold high-end production guidance (alongside the market’s estimates) by ~6.74% and ~22.41% for other metals, with silver production near the top of the expectations, for a beat of ~3.23% on a GEO basis.

We can see a significant jump in FCF as a result of the increase in production and especially commodity prices, with significant investments in mineral stream interests - which more than doubled to $1.34 billion - while the dividend was a very sustainable ~$296.37 million last year and recently got increased by ~18%, for a ~0.57% yield.

WPM’s growth pipeline remains one of the main advantages compared to peers, with several investments made in recent history fueling their near-term growth, expected to expand their GEO (gold equivalent ounce) production by ~50% by 2030. They expect to maintain attributable production at ~1.2 million GEOs, while maintaining their majority gold-silver exposure, which also sets them apart from most other peers.

As a note, especially given the kind of money they and the miners get in the current environment, it’s very likely to see many other opportunities that can help them grow this pipeline beyond 2030.

For 2026, they expect their production to continue growing especially in terms of silver, reaching 860,000 to 940,000 GEOs, with their pipeline advancing towards their long-term goal, with boosts coming from projects such as Blackwater, Mineral Park, Fenix, Hemlo, Goose or Platreef helping offset the fall in Constancia and a slight decrease at their flagship Salobo following a peak year.

Financially, based on WPM’s latest report, they remain in a very strong position that’s common for companies in the industry (especially following the jump in gold and silver prices), with current assets covering their total liabilities, although this is before they closed a major deal that I’ll mention below, which adds a bit of leverage but is still far from an issue from a financial point of view.

The BHP Deal

A few days ago, the company’s CEO was officially replaced, stepping down after more than 15 years of leading the business (will hold a position in the Board to assure the smooth transition). However, he didn’t leave until adding what others and I consider a bad deal to the company’s portfolio - despite his strong track record of building this into an industry powerhouse - the BHP (BHP) deal where they give the miner $4.3 billion upfront and pay 20% of the spot price for a 33.75% silver stream in the major Antamina project in Peru. This will be funded through a mix of cash on hand and debt, as you can see in the image above.

Fellow analyst Trapping Value modeled the numbers behind this transaction in a recent article dedicated to it, and this does look like a terrible deal unless we see a major fundamental jump in silver prices (XAGUSD:CUR) or they assumed a much lower discount rate, which to me looks like buying into significant FOMO at overhyped prices driven by trader sentiment rather than fundamentals, which is a big issue if that’s most of what they do with the significant gains seen from the current bull run.

They announced other deals in the meantime (including its entry into Australia after the deal with KGL), but I’ll also note that the company paid $4.3 billion for the rights to 90% of BHP’s 33.75% of the silver (drops to 22.5% for the remainder of the mine life after 100 million ounces are delivered) after already owning 33.75% tied to their 2015 deal with Glencore (GLCNF) - for which they paid $900 million, further emphasizing what might as well be buying at a terrible time.

Not only did they pay a fraction (~20.9%) for a similar deal back then, but the deal with Glencore is actually superior since the stream drops to 22.5% after 140 Moz, not 100 Moz like this one, and the Glencore one uses a 100% payable rate compared to 90% for the BHP deal.

This is the difference between the kind of deal that made Wheaton what it is today (closed at very low prices when miners needed the money) and what buying into the market’s hype looks like. Sure, if silver is getting a significant repricing, then this deal makes sense and it can be quite profitable, but I believe the company had the kind of leverage, power and size needed to find better ones and not take this kind of risk when commodity prices skyrocketed - especially since they're putting even more eggs in an already large basket since they had a big stream here already.

Potential Long-Term PM Repricing

The main factor behind the recent volatility is the ongoing Iran conflict, and even though it may be counterintuitive to see precious metals falling as a result of a significant macro conflict that can destabilize the global economy, this is in part the result of a flight to safety into the dollar (and potential profit-taking in precious metals).

This pushes the price of these commodities down in dollar terms alongside treasuries, boosting treasury yields and potentially leading to even more demand for the dollar since these notes are now offering more attractive yields that can help shield money away from the potential inflation and fear.

I highlighted before how the recent jump in precious metals was mostly the result of a massive jump in demand coming from investing, while their other sources of demand (e.g. banks, jewelry especially for gold) dropped or was relatively stable, which I believe comes as a double-edged blade. As seen over the past years, a supply deficit was not enough to cause major jumps, as silver for example has been running on a deficit for over half a decade now based on data from the Silver Institute and peers.

This can be a major reason why we see the significant jumps in these commodities over the past months and at least in the near-term, as the more “rational” demand fell while leaving more room to speculative, FOMO, hype, etc. demand, which is not the worst thing since it can open some opportunities such as several I highlighted in gold mining recently.

Again, this is not a matter of phones or other electronics using less silver, for example, but the current macro pressure combined with the fact that higher material prices will mostly be passed to the final consumer to maintain margins can result in people not affording that phone anymore or delaying the acquisition to instead focus on paying rent.

However, this may not last for that long, as I don’t expect the the ongoing Iran conflict to last for a prolonged period (detailed why here), and even though we might see a period of pressure in the near-term due to the inflationary pressure that can delay rate cuts or even lead to hikes - which can further strengthen the dollar - the fundamentals remain in favor of a repricing.

Once this conflict is over and the previous trajectory recovers, the market may change its expectations and the opposite of the current flight to safety into the dollar can revert, while the previous issues (e.g., rising debt, economic/political uncertainty, Japan’s potential treasury selloff, etc.) can drive the narrative again, potentially supporting higher prices going forward, especially after all the money printing.

Meanwhile, silver can also benefit from several long-term trends especially in AI/electrification - unlike gold - which adds to the metal’s potential beyond its precious metal status and could help ensure solid levels of demand.

Risks Reiterated

I’ll highlight once again the geography risks that WPM comes with, as we’re talking about a company with significant exposure to countries like Brazil, Peru or Mexico that the market currently trades at terrible discount rates, which is not reflected in WPM’s price despite the risk being relatively high compared to peers. As a note, Vale’s (VALE) Salobo mine in Brazil represented roughly 37% of the company’s production mix in 2025, and even once we account for all the diversification by 2030, that’s still estimated to represent 26% by that time, while Antamina would represent another 18% combined.

Meanwhile, especially given the new supply-demand dynamics at least in the near-term, it wouldn’t be impossible to see more volatility, and even though that won’t be enough to affect WPM as a company that much unless we’re talking about major drops, the stock can continue to be volatile.

The company’s impressive growth can also be hurt by potential delays, and I also noted in more detail recently how future production is (and should be) discounted by the market, which is another thing that’s not that visible here.

Valuation

For WPM's DCF to Equity, using the same realized prices as in 2025 as a base (for a blended GEO of $3,554/oz) and an average cash cost of $514, let's assume the company's operating margins will be $3,040 throughout the cycle, which assumes lower prices than today. Given their balance sheet, I'll also not account for any changes in net borrowings, to highlight their organic potential.

Based on their production guidance, we would get to a FCF of $2.63 billion in 2026, $2.85 billion in 2027, $3.07 billion in 2028, $3.29 billion in 2029 (assuming that they're adding 75,000 GEO per year from 2026 to 2030) and $3.51 billion in 2030, which I'll assume can grow at a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years from potential macro improvements (above their flat estimate) and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with what I consider an appropriate discount rate of 8% to take into account their great quality but also the level of risk their portfolio comes with, as well as 454.84 million diluted shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

Note that I’m assuming for a 3% CAGR from potential improvements for 2031-2035 without assuming any investments in mineral stream interests, to be extra generous to account for potential new discoveries or other accretive factors.

Note that a 3% CAGR may not look like much, but over 5 years it's almost the same as adding a new Antamina in terms of production. If we wanted to account for growth CAPEX (investments in stream interests in this case), it should’ve been discounted from the FCF.

This gets us to an equity value of $57.63 billion, for an intrinsic value of $126.71, which is below the current $135.56 at the moment of writing this. This would place their P/FCF multiple at about 18.6x based on the year 10 FCF and the terminal value, which I also consider fair for a company like this, as we're basically not assuming any growth CAPEX (in this case, that would be the investments in mineral interests).

Conclusions

Although they’re trading above my intrinsic fair value estimate that already assumes that generous 3% CAGR despite using a model that theoretically highlights their organic potential from this state onwards (without investments), I’ll rate WPM a Hold, as their quality and higher prices in the future can justify a price like the one we see today.

However, that’s not to say there aren’t risks. The company’s exposure to what the market generally considers very risky countries when it comes to virtually any other investment (look at the P/FCF Vale usually trades at), and the asset concentration after the BHP deal is not getting much better, while the ongoing macro uncertainty and significant speculative demand can add even more levels of volatility and uncertainty.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: March Jobs Report: Significant Bounce Back, Health Care Sector Leads
data.description:
In an otherwise quiet day, with U.S. equity markets closed for the holiday weekend, Friday’s job report provided some good news to investors juggling continuing market volatility.

On the final trading day prior to the holiday, indexes reversed course on two-day momentum following President Trump’s speech on the war with Iran on Wednesday evening.

Following that speech, all three indexes remain in the red on a YTD basis, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI), S&P 500 (SPY), and Nasdaq Composite (NDX), all down between 3% and 5%. Yields on long-term yields, meanwhile, continue to track within the 4.35% to 4.4% range.

With the holiday closure, investors will have to wait until Monday to express their reaction to today’s report. In my view, attention likely will have already pivoted back to developments on the war front by then.

Today’s report was a positive bounce back reading from a February reading that was somewhat distorted due to the healthcare strike. Here’s everything to know about the March jobs report.

What Sectors Added Jobs In March?

On an overall basis, employers added 178,000 jobs in March, well above the 59,000 jobs expected. Job gains were strong throughout but most pronounced in the healthcare sector.

After losing jobs in February due to strike activity, the sector bounced back in a big way. The sector added 76,000 jobs during the month, well above the twelve month average of 29,000 jobs.

This wasn't a surprise to me, especially with the strike activity concluding. Gains, however, were better than expected elsewhere.

In the construction sector, for example, employers added 26,000 jobs. This was almost half the gains that were expected for the month. These gains were supplemented by a 21,000 job gain in the transportation and warehousing sector as well.

Two struggling sectors continue to be federal, which lost 18,000 jobs and financial activities, which saw payrolls decline 15,000. The latter could be the impacts rippling across financial services, with recently announced layoffs at companies such as Morgan Stanley (MS).

Unemployment and Wage Growth Trend

With the unemployment rate landing at 4.3%, the figure is largely in line with what we’ve seen over the past several months.

In my view, the labor market appears to remain in a narrow holding pattern. While the modest decline from February’s level is worth calling out, it does little to change the broader narrative that has defined this cycle.

Employers continue to show a clear hesitancy to meaningfully expand payrolls, but they appear to be showing an equally strong reluctance to part ways with existing workers on a large scale.

That push and pull has kept unemployment confined to a tight range even as overall job growth has cooled from prior years.

To me, I believe businesses remain in a wait-and-see mode. However, with more companies quietly announcing workforce reductions, it’s possible and maybe even likely that these cuts begin to surface more clearly in the data in the months ahead.

Fed Policy Implications

Much like in February, I don’t believe March’s jobs data will materially shift the near-term outlook for the Fed. If anything, market expectations have drifted even further toward the idea that policymakers could remain on hold for the entirety of 2026.

In my view, the logic is fairly straightforward. It now takes far fewer monthly job gains to keep the unemployment rate steady than it did even a year ago.

With the breakeven level for payroll growth estimated to be a fraction of prior thresholds, a report showing, say 50K to 70K jobs, would likely no longer be viewed as weak enough to force the Fed’s hand.

At the same time, however, geopolitical uncertainty, particularly surrounding the conflict with Iran, adds another layer of caution for policymakers.

I tend to agree with the growing market view. Unless unemployment begins to move meaningfully higher, I believe the Fed has ample room to remain steady and keep rates unchanged for longer than many initially expected.

Final Thoughts On The March Jobs Report

Today’s release was a good report for Good Friday.

The headline number showed a significant rebound from February’s distortion, healthcare hiring returned, and the unemployment rate remained pinned in its familiar mid-4% range.

With U.S. equity markets closed Friday, any true market reaction won’t occur until Monday. By then, however, I suspect most investors will have already shifted their focus back to developments surrounding the war with Iran rather than dissecting the finer details of today's payroll print, which was mostly a 'bounce back' reading.

That dynamic speaks to the broader investor view I outlined in my market outlook article from December 2025.

That is, selectivity likely remains paramount through 2026. Geopolitical conflicts, uneven sector hiring, and a Federal Reserve likely content to sit on its hands create an environment where momentum may struggle to do the heavy lifting.

For investors, that means continuing to be deliberate, selective, and disciplined in where capital is deployed.

data.title: Barrick: You Haven't Seen Anything Yet
data.description:
I Believe Barrick Is A Great "Buy"

Barrick Mining Corporation (B) rebranded from Barrick Gold Corporation in May 2025 to reflect its growing dual-commodity focus - the "gold" in the company's name isn't enough to truly capture Barrick's operating activities now. Anyway, like many other gold stocks, Barrick has gone through a massive rally throughout 2025 amid the rise in commodity prices, but then it started to fall sharply when the underlying commodity began to weaken (since March 2026).

In my opinion, it's all just market noise because even if the gold and copper prices continue to correct (to some extent), the fundamentals should remain strong. Barrick has one of the strongest balance sheets in the industry, which is essential for a miner, and with the upcoming corporate events that I'm anticipating, the stock should reprice higher over the next few months.

Why Do I Think So?

Thanks to the 25% QoQ production ramp at the Carlin complex within the Nevada Gold Mines joint venture, the firm's gold production went up by 5% QoQ to 871,000 ounces. Copper production rose 13% QoQ and reached 62,000 metric tons. The selling prices were quite high for both gold ($4,177 per ounce) and copper ($5.42 per pound) in Q4, so, as a result, Barrick has managed to generate $6 billion in quarterly revenues, which was 45% above last year's figure, and it beat the consensus estimate by over 16% (that looks like the biggest beat since at least 2022, according to Seeking Alpha). The bottom line came in at $1.04 per share (in U.S. dollars), which is a 126% YoY growth, and it's above the consensus by almost 19%.

Operating cash flows for the quarters amounted to $2.73 billion, which brought in a massive $1.62 billion in FCF in Q4 alone. The new shareholders' return framework that the management announced is targeting 50% for FCFs to investors on an annualized basis, so the base quarterly dividend rose by 40%. Also, Barrick repurchased ~$500 million worth of its own shares during Q4 alone, and I think they're likely to keep accumulating more shares in the next quarters as the production scales further.

Barrick regained operational control of the Loulo-Gounkoto complex in Mali in late November 2025. It's Barrick's most profitable African mine, which is now going to contribute 260,000-290,000 ounces in 2026, heavily supporting the FCF generation. Compared to 2025, it's going to be an 8-10x increase in production from this project. In addition to that, Barrick keeps working on a massive plant expansion and mine life extension at Pueblo Viejo, which can help maintain an annual production of 800,000 ounces through the 2040s. I think these near-term catalysts should help Barrick beat the current FY2026-2027 consensus estimates.

Longer-term, I like the odds of a copper supercycle and the fact that Barrick already has 2 transformational copper assets that can add a lot of value in 2028-2029. For example, they're working on a $2 billion Lumwana Super Pit Expansion plant in Zambia that will double capacity by 2028, becoming a top-25 copper-producing mine in the world. The Reko Diq copper-gold deposit was initially planned to come online in 2028, but because of the security risks in the Middle East and the Balochistan province, the likely first production will be in 2029. It's a long-term opportunity for Barrick with the copper market slowly rolling to a structural deficit throughout 2026-2028, mainly because of electrification (EVs, renewables, grid spending, etc.). UBS analysts see limited growth in supplies in 2026 (proprietary source), so I guess the pricing environment should shift to the bulls' side, benefiting those who hold considerable assets in this mining niche (just like Barrick). Once the U.S.-Iran de-escalation comes, and we see limited supply growth in 2026-2028, Barrick will be a clear winner in this setup - the market is very likely to reprice both the earnings expectations and the valuation multiples.

We continue to expect the copper market to move into a modest deficit in 2026, widening through 2027-28, underpinned by resilient grid investment and broader energy-transition demand. While our 2026 copper demand forecast has been revised down to +2% to reflect heightened economic uncertainty, we continue to expect grid spending and other energy-transition demand drivers - such as electric vehicles, battery storage systems, and renewables - to remain resilient or potentially benefit from higher energy prices.

On the supply side, the outlook for mine production has not materially changed. We continue to expect limited growth in 2026, resulting in less than 1% growth in refined copper supply. While softer demand and higher inventories could delay the onset of market tightness, we remain constructive on the underlying fundamentals and expect tightening conditions to support higher copper prices over time. Should a de-escalation scenario materialize, we might look to increase exposure to high-quality copper miners.

Source: UBS note [April 2026].

Coming back to the shorter-term catalysts, it's impossible to ignore the upcoming separation of Barrick's safe-jurisdiction North American assets from its higher-risk international operations in Africa and Asia, which can unlock a lot of value for investors. Here's how my friend, Oakoff Investments, put it in his recent article on B:

By spinning off a 10-15% minority stake in this "NewCo," Barrick wants to unlock a ~$42 billion valuation for its Tier 1 assets, thus attracting a premium valuation from investors who desire US-only gold exposure.

Right now, B trades at only 4.88x forward EV/EBITDA multiple, per Seeking Alpha, and it's a very low figure on both an absolute basis and compared to most firms within the Materials sector. The "NewCo" can easily have an EBITDA multiple in the 15-18x range, completely closing the current valuation gap.

So, if the market assigns a $42 billion valuation to NewCo, and the parent company retains a massive controlling interest of 85-90%, there should be a massive repricing to the upside, in my view.

Source: Oakoff's article.

Indeed, the B stock is trading at one of the lowest valuation multiples compared to its same-sized peers these days:

Compared to the average of its group, B was 13-14% undervalued in mid-March, per UBS's report [proprietary source], and I guess the undervaluation has only widened as B kept underperforming on a relative basis.

Argus Research's analysts [proprietary source] see a fair value per share of $50, which is 20% above today's stock price:

I wouldn't value B based on its P/S, but rather on the P/E basis. Assuming that Barrick beats the current FY2027 EPS consensus by 10%, which is a reasonable assumption given the latest beats, and if Barrick trades at only 12x earnings within the next 12 months, the price target should be close to $58/share. My PT is only $1 above the consensus PT of $57, based on Seeking Alpha data:

Given all this, I believe Barrick deserves a Buy rating on its latest dip.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

I may be wrong in my bullish thesis on Barrick because there are plenty of risks to its future operations. Mining is usually a very risky place for investors. In B's case, after Newmont Corporation (NEM) issued a formal notice of default to Barrick regarding their NGM JV, it triggered a 30-day remedy period, which threatens to freeze key decision-making. This JV generates ~60% of Barrick's total market value. Major corporate actions like this might lead to a delay of the planned IPO, so investors might not get the long-awaited catalyst in time.

It's also important to remember geopolitical risks, especially in today's macro environment. Barrick's operations, and the key plants I discussed today, are placed in the regions with substantial sovereign and operational risks, such as Pakistan, Mali, or Tanzania.

Plus, one of the reasons for Barrick's correction on a YTD basis was the management's weaker-than-expected guidance for FY2026 that foresees higher all-in sustaining costs and lower gold production levels. So, my core assumption that Barrick should beat the upcoming quarters' consensus estimates may turn out to be off.

The Bottom Line

Despite the risks, I like the fact that Barrick is repositioning into a dual-metal-focused miner that is very likely to enjoy the possible supercycle in copper in the next 2-3 years. The upcoming North American IPO in late 2026 should unlock value and lead to a likely bullish repricing. My valuation calculations point to an upside of 39-40%, and Wall Street analysts seem to agree with me. I rate the B stock as a Buy.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: SoFi: Market Panic Just Created An Amazing Entry Point
data.description:
SoFi: I didn't expect this >50% decline

A 55% decline in the stock of SoFi Technologies (SOFI).

No matter how I layer it or spin it, or inject euphemism to mitigate the downside bias, the fact is that significant damage has already been done to SOFI’s bullish sentiments.

By the time you read my update right now, as I try to get your attention in the midst of the financial sector facing multitudinous headwinds, you should also know that SOFI appears to be forming a bottom, somewhere over the $15 level.

Now, just to be clear. I'm not saying that there is no chance that SOFI will definitely not drop further.

After all, SOFI is by no means valued at a discount, and that's not even close.

Compared to the sector median of 10x, SOFI’s valuation (27x forward P/E) has really decelerated and compressed significantly over the past few months.

But, it's still way above that of its high-growth fintech peers, such as Affirm (AFRM), rated at 12.5x forward earnings, and Upstart (UPST), slotted in at just under 11.3x forward P/E.

SoFi: Facing more troubling stress sensors in the financial sector now

In an otherwise less onerous economic climate where risks of a recession are decidedly lower, I believe investors might be willing to pay up to own SoFi.

After all, the company isn't going for the most financially stressed customers in the economy. Back in my previous SoFi writeup, I explained why I think the firm’s robust execution across its various business segments has proved to me that its flywheel is working. And that should help keep investor confidence high in the stock.

However, the sprawling challenges that afflicted the financial sector recently have also affected the market’s confidence in the sector.

And, being a stock valued at a relative premium, it didn't surprise me that SOFI took the brunt of the selling pressure, as the market readjusted their outlook, compounded by the downside risks emanating from the ongoing Iranian conflict with the US.

As a reminder, lending remains the most significant segment of SoFi's growing business.

Despite having expended significant efforts to convince investors that the company is moving steadily away toward a less volatile, and more predictable fee based income stream.

However, the fact that SoFi still relies mainly on lending (>50%) for its overall net revenue is a solemn reminder that until the company completely or significantly transitions away from lending as the main source of its business model, the market will continue to be skeptical.

Especially when we might have to deal with a potential implosion (worst case scenario) in the financial sector, or a weakening in America's consumer economy. Consumer confidence is already taking a tumble, despite recent optimism.

The ongoing Iranian War expected to slow the US economy after having overcome the headwinds from Trump’s tariffs.

Questions relating to financial sector growth stocks like SoFi will become front and center for the rest of the year.

And we might have to move along with these ambiguities until we have more resolution over whether a more adverse outcome from the burgeoning private credit turmoil could spill over to stymie investor confidence, leading them to cut financial sector exposure markedly, and deepening SOFI’s slump as a result.

SoFi: Cross selling opportunities are key to its business model transition

Nevertheless, I guess some of us could point out that SoFi does possess a relatively high quality lending portfolio.

After all, it posted that its lending business had a “segment weighted average origination FICO score of 749” for FY2025.

And SoFi relies on the Financial Services Productivity Loop flywheel for cross selling opportunities to raise awareness and lift higher revenue breeding prospects.

I believe it strengthens the narrative that SOFI is inculcating a robust customer base that should help it circumvent some of the most pressing challenges that could affect its lending business in a prolonged downturn.

However, it’s also undeniable that SoFi’s recent growth prospects rely on a suite of third party partners through its loan platform business to spur growth.

At the same time, it has also allowed the company to transition more confidently and rapidly into a fee based model, although it remains to be seen whether such momentum can still be maintained in the downcycle.

These are key risk factors that the company clearly attempted to caution investors about, especially for those of you who think that the current derisking isn’t tantamount to a structural downturn that could throttle the US economy and send us into a stagflationary scenario.

I think what's clear is that while risks are rising, such a downcast outlook doesn't look like the base case for now, and therefore, doesn’t deserve such a pessimistic posture, does it?

Still, I could understand why investors chose to take profit quickly, as I also explained in a recent Market Outlook on how there is no easy off ramp from the Middle Eastern hostilities in the near term.

SoFi: Unlikely to face systemic risks of a deepening fallout

And chances are, the possibilities for a deepening conflict are not remote.

In that case, I believe the market is looking at the outside case that interest rate increases are no longer out of the picture through the rest of 2026.

The likelihood of a near term raise may not be contemplated yet (see commentary above), as monetary policymakers stay cautious and allow the data to print first.

In other words, I guess because some of the tailwinds that were linked to a more dovish Federal Reserve have likely been cast aside. That will definitely be a big disappointment for the growth rated SOFI stock, as interest rate tailwinds could very well become headwinds if the situation in the Strait of Hormuz worsens.

Therefore, it's imperative that the risks linked to the private credit space continue to be capped, and not lead to a systemic fallout that could throttle confidence all over the sector.

Such a ripple effect could hammer SOFI hard as well, as financial sector investors head for the exit doors.

For now, it doesn’t look like the risks could overwhelm us, although the private credit reaction appears to be getting worse, as observed with the situation engulfing Blue Owl Capital (OWL) this week.

SoFi made clear mentions of interest rate related challenges that hobbled its business in 2022/23, which is common sense.

It indicated that “our business is sensitive to interest rates and interest rates are highly sensitive to many factors that are beyond our control.”

That’s true because SoFi is a price taker here, and while it could shield itself by focusing on the more affluent customers, it’s not immune to potentially lower revenue opportunities if originations were to decline.

Thus, there's a distinct possibility that it could hamper the market’s expectations of its earnings growth cadence for the next few years.

And then, when we consider that SoFi’s loan platform business, or LPB, hasn't quite been tested in a downcycle environment, it further compounds the risk that investors must price in, complicating the bullish thesis for the company.

SoFi: EPS outlook still looks solid

That said, am I about to throw in the towel for SOFI right now? Not quite.

Based on a cursory glance against SOFI’s peers, the company is still expected to deliver an earnings growth CAGR close to the 40% mark.

It’s a clear standout prognosis as compared to its peers.

I guess that's why the market was prepared to give it a premium valuation, even though it has clearly fallen significantly.

The derating makes sense as investors start to price in the challenges that I explained earlier.

The question is whether SOFI management has the conviction to see this through.

And also whether the private credit fallout could lead to something worse, as growth related plays will tend to be hit harder as investors flee.

In this case, I think CEO Anthony Noto’s recent insider purchases demonstrate a high level of conviction in the stock in the current context, while the company also faces short seller allegations that raised downside volatility recently.

Although I must highlight that the stock has been consolidating over the past 2 months, indicating that the selling pressure has somewhat subsided, and possibly even bottomed if the market has largely priced in these challenges.

Morningstar’s latest sector valuation estimates also show that the financial sector is now >8% undervalued, entering undervaluation territory for the first time since early 2024.

It marks a notable development that also caught the eyes of financial sector insiders (XLF) as they ramped up their purchases, despite the scrutiny that is going on right now.

I think we can clearly glean from the financial insiders buy/sell ratio that has been creeping up despite the somber media narratives percolating.

If you're telling me that we are at the cusp of a financial crisis or GFC 2.0, insiders are clearly not showing that with their actions, right?

There’s little conviction in such a gloomy trajectory.

Buy Or Sell SOFI Stock?

And that’s important for SoFi, as it needs a more stable climate to outshine, and not face a deepening market reckoning that could send its valuation further down south.

I think the key to the bullish observation is that SOFI has suffered tremendous volatility before, so the recent slide over the past few months isn't something new for experienced investors.

Importantly, I believe as long as SOFI buyers are determined to hang on to the $15 level for the next few weeks, we will likely see the stock engage more affirmatively and switch back to an advancing phase, as momentum buyers return after the onslaught.

Will that definitely pan out? Obviously no guarantees of anything.

But, we must always keep our eyes on the trend.

And I believe you could see clearly that the trend is still actually in favour of SOFI bulls (uptrending bias).

I have confidence that the current negative news cycle right now is a near term scare in the financial sector, but not a debilitating downturn.

This represents an amazing opportunity for those of you who didn't manage to go on board, as SOFI makes a turnaround to pick up those who missed the bus earlier.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Construction Partners: It's Time To Correct A Big Mistake (Upgrade)
data.description:
I have a very good track record when it comes to the market. But nobody's perfect. One of my biggest flaws as an investor is that I sometimes do not give enough weight to the prospect of growth. I am, after all, a value investor. So I am incredibly sensitive to the price that I am paying for the value that I am receiving. When there are instances where a high quality growth company offers potential upside but at a price, I tend to err on the side of caution. Often, this allows me to avoid costly mistakes. But other times, the end result is missed upside.

One of the best examples of this can be seen by looking at Construction Partners, Inc. (ROAD), a rather interesting business that operates as a civil infrastructure company that focuses on construction and maintenance activities for roadways in the markets in which it operates. At one point, I had been bullish about the company. And during that time, shares rose 62.5%, far outpacing the 13.6% increase that the S&P 500 (SP500) saw over the same window of time. I eventually downgraded the stock in May of 2024. The Hold rating that I assigned it then, and that I reaffirmed in October of that year, was based on the view that, even though the company had been doing well to grow, shares were priced at levels that looked expensive. But since then, the stock has performed remarkably well, skyrocketing 101.1%. That's almost four times as much as the 28.5% increase that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. Looking at the data now, I regret that decision. And with new data now available, I actually think that upgrading it to a soft Buy, even though the stock is fair value at the moment, is wholly appropriate.

A wild ride down the road to growth

Over the last few years now, Construction Partners has achieved exceptional growth. From 2023 through 2025, revenue nearly doubled from $1.56 billion to $2.81 billion. As revenue grew, profitability skyrocketed. The chart above shows net income, adjusted net income, and cash flows, all rising drastically. Perhaps the most impressive is just how strong cash flow growth has been, with EBITDA for the company soaring from $172.6 million to $423.7 million. The chart below, meanwhile, shows results for the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same time of 2025. Revenue of $809.5 million ended up being 44.1% above the $561.6 million that the company reported a year earlier. Profitability rose drastically as well.

Although the company has benefited from organic growth, we should not understate just how important acquisition activities have been to it. In the chart below, you can see how much the firm has spent on acquisitions over the last three fiscal years, as well as for the first quarter of 2026. Over this window of time, the firm has allocated just cash alone toward purchases of $1.69 billion. For a business with a market capitalization of $5.98 billion, that is significant. Some of the latest purchases have been, in October of last year, its acquisition of certain asphalt manufacturing and construction assets from affiliates of Vulcan Materials Company (VMC) in a deal worth $108.4 million, as well as its purchase in the same month of P&S Paving, which is an asphalt manufacturing and construction business based out of Florida, in exchange for $88.2 million of cash and $51.5 million of stock. Some purchases have been even larger. In November of 2024, as an example, the company bought up Lone Star Paving, which management described as a vertically integrated asphalt manufacturing and paving company out of Texas. That cost us $659 million in cash, plus shares valued at that time at $236.3 million. There were some other items included in this as well, worth approximately $111.5 million.

Of course, acquisition activities like this require significant capital. Some companies achieve this by issuing additional shares. And as I mentioned in the previous paragraph, there were some shares issued for some of its purchases. However, a lot of what the company has done has been fueled by debt. In 2023, the firm had just $326 million of net debt. But by the end of the first quarter of 2026, that had ballooned to $1.64 billion. Unfortunately, this has meant a rise in the net leverage ratio of the business, with that figure growing from 1.89 to 3.43 over the last three years. And if we use the net debt figure for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, and estimated EBITDA for this year, that would mean a net leverage ratio of about 3.02. However, it is likely that additional debt will be assumed over the next several months. Plus, the rapid rate of these acquisitions has made relying on the net leverage ratio in this capacity questionable since debt is added on to the balance sheet immediately while EBITDA is incurred over time.

Management certainly seems optimistic when it comes to the 2026 fiscal year. They believe that revenue will grow from $2.81 billion last year to between $3.48 billion and $3.56 billion this year. Some of this will almost certainly be organic in nature. After all, between the first quarter of 2025 in the first quarter of 2026, revenue expanded $247.9 million. $19.7 million of that was organic growth from existing markets thanks to strong demand in both the public and private spaces. But $228.2 million of this revenue expansion was thanks to acquisition activities that the company engaged in. So certainly, most of this upside would be because of the steps that management has been taking to grow the business. And those steps continue through this day. On April 1st, for instance, the company said that it completed its purchase of Four Star Paving, a premier commercial paving contractor based out of Tennessee. And in early February, the company completed its acquisition of GMJ Paving Company, which serves as an asphalt paving contractor for public infrastructure projects that is based in Texas.

Speaking of Texas, management has been adamant about focusing its efforts on metropolitan statistical areas that are located in some of the fastest growing parts of the nation. Many of these are located in the Sunbelt region, which is doing really well because of migration from other states. Texas alone is a very interesting opportunity for the business. When management gave their investor day presentation in October of last year, they pointed out that Texas alone has the most lane miles and largest transportation program in the U.S. State and local government awards along these lines totaled $17.4 billion in 2024. That was $7.7 billion more than what was ultimately allocated in Florida, which was the second highest state. This opens the door to continued organic growth as well. In fact, management estimated that, between 2026 and 2030, organic revenue growth should be around 7% to 8% annually. Naturally, it's not just Texas either. In fact, the company operates in 6 of the 10 states with the highest population growth in the US. This would be Texas, Florida, North Carolina, Georgia, South Carolina, and Tennessee.

Continued reliance on leverage does create certain risks over time. But management has made clear that, by the year 2030, the company intends to reduce its net leverage ratio to between 1.5 and 2.5. By that year, revenue should be around $6.03 billion. And as the company continues to expand, its EBITDA margins should grow also. This year, it is likely to be between 15% and 15.8%. But by 2030, it should expand to 17%. This translates to EBITDA, at the midpoint, of $1.03 billion. And according to management, this should result in operating cash flow of somewhere around $820.1 million at the midpoint. Scaling adjusted net profits up similarly should give us a reading for that year of $325.5 million.

In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced using these estimates, as well as estimates for 2026 and historical results for 2025. The 2025 and 2026 valuation figures certainly don't paint this as an overvalued growth play. Instead, they paint the company as a firm that is pretty much fairly valued. In the chart below, meanwhile, you can see how it stacks up against five other similar firms. Its pricing is actually comparable to these other businesses if you ask me. So even on a relative basis, we aren't looking at a business that is overpriced.

In the event that management can achieve the objectives that they have set for 2030, there could be some meaningful upside here. In the chart below, you can see annualized upside from today through the end of the year 2030 under a few different scenarios. For starters, I decided to assume that, in 2030, the business will still trade to the same multiples it's trading at today. This would imply upside on an annual basis so between 22.9% and 23.6%. In the event that it instead trades at multiples that match the average of these other comparable firms, the range does get quite a bit larger. But even in the worst case scenario, we would be looking at upside of about 13.6% annually. And in the best case, it would be 22.5%.

What is very noteworthy here is that, with the exception of one of the scenarios, the biggest annualized spread that we are looking at here is only about 2.5% per annum. So if we take the 13.6% annualized upside out as an outlier, and even if the firm achieves just half of the upside coming from these other approaches, then you're looking at a return that pretty much matches or slightly exceeds with the stock market should get over that window of time. What this tells me is that the risk to reward scenario here is certainly in the favor of investors. Even if management really messes up between now and then, there is a high probability it at least matches the market. And in all likelihood, given its track record, it will exceed it. Perhaps substantially so.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my belief that I was premature in downgrading Construction Partners a couple of years back. Management has clearly done a fantastic job of expanding the business, though we do need to keep in mind that the net leverage ratio is currently quite a bit higher than what management intends for the end of this decade. Assuming that the business does not make any big mistakes, I see no reason to believe that investors have an unfavorable scenario here. In fact, I would say that this creates a favorable risk to reward outcome. And that leads me to upgrade it to a soft Buy.

data.title: Oil And War Panic Has Created Dirt Cheap Picks, Up To 14% Yields
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

"Be fearful when others are greedy, and greedy when others are fearful"

– Warren Buffett

This is one of Buffett's more famous quotes, which is excellent investment advice that the vast majority of investors will fail to implement. The problem is that fear often exists for very good reasons.

As I write this, the market is fearful: Source

It is fearful for several good reasons. The escalation of war in Iran, the risk to the global oil supply, and the fear of recession are all valid and reasonable fears. Combine that with large-cap stocks trading at very high valuations, and it is reasonable to believe that there is a risk of a crash severe enough for us to name it. Source

Today, let's take a look at two holdings that I'm buying while the market gets fearful.

Pick #1: AGNC – Yield 14.4%

AGNC Investment Corporation (AGNC) is a mortgage REIT that focuses on agency MBS, which are mortgage-backed securities where Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac guarantees the mortgages. If a borrower defaults, the agencies buy back the mortgages at par.

AGNC uses leverage, and it profits from the difference between its assets and its cost of funds. The path to cash flow is clearly laid out on this slide: Source

You have the average asset yield, which is what AGNC collects. It was at 4.91% at the end of Q4. Then you have the cost of funds, which is 3.10%. That's what AGNC is paying in interest. Take the asset yield 4.91% minus the cost of funds of 3.10%, and you get the net interest spread of 1.81%.

The higher the net interest spread, the more money AGNC is making on its investments.

Recently, we saw AGNC's price come down. This is likely because AGNC's book value has declined. Here is the 5.5% coupon agency MBS price: Source

This is in response to U.S. Treasury rates rising as the market starts to fear inflation being driven by oil prices. For AGNC's book value, this is a negative because the vast majority of their assets are agency MBS, and if MBS prices decline, asset prices decline while debt remains the same. That translates to lower book value.

What does it mean for cash flow?

Well, during the Fed hiking cycle, we saw both book value and cash flow decline. The reason is that long-term rates went up, reducing the value of assets, and simultaneously, short-term rates went up, increasing the cost of funds. In the chart above, we can see that the average asset yield went from 4.47% to 4.91% from Q4 2023 to Q4 2025. Meanwhile, the cost of funds went up from 1.39% to 3.10%.

What's happening to the cost of funds today?

It's essentially flat. The cost of funds is tied directly to the Federal Reserve Target Rate, which was left unchanged. SOFR (Secured Overnight Financing Rate) hit 3.62% on March 19 – a cycle low: Source

So, while asset prices coming down is a negative for book value, when MBS prices come down (yield goes up), that means AGNC can invest in higher-coupon MBS. With the 5.5% coupons trading near par, that means AGNC can increase its average yield by taking the principal payments from holdings that were averaging 4.91% yield into investments that are yielding 5.5%. At the same time, their cost of funds will remain relatively stable.

It should be noted that AGNC's cost of funds is going to increase a little, because it is artificially low through interest rate swaps.

They only have $7.3 billion (approximately 11%) of swaps that will mature in 2026. But that is something that was going to occur regardless of whether MBS prices went up or down.

AGNC has been trading at a premium to book value. In recent earnings, they have been taking advantage of that to issue equity. AGNC should be issuing equity now to take advantage of being able to buy higher-coupon MBS while prices are low.

The bottom line is that this type of movement is going to cause book value to decline, but it has a positive impact on cash flow. It's especially true for the mREITs that are trading at a premium and can raise capital to buy the dip.

The risk for AGNC would be that the Federal Reserve decides to start hiking rates. Then we see a return of the trend we saw in the last hiking cycle. However, we don't find that theory to be supported by history. Oil price spikes usually result in a slowing economy. Raising interest rates isn't going to reduce oil prices or inflation caused by high oil prices. There is a distinct difference between inflation caused by a lack of supply and inflation caused by excessive demand. Raising rates helps reduce excessive demand; it doesn't cause new oil to appear on the market. More likely, if oil prices remain high enough to materially impact inflation, we will see the onset of a recession, and the Fed will be talking about cutting, not raising.

Pick #2: O – Yield 5.2%

When fear, uncertainty, and doubt increase in the market, it is good to have your "go-tos," companies that are so strong that you can have a high degree of confidence that they will navigate any situation. Now these companies will be subjected to price swings like any other investment. That's just the reality of publicly traded stocks. Large price swings are normal and unavoidable.

We're talking about investments that you don't have to spend time worrying about in a crisis because you know:

  1. They have a fortress balance sheet. A strong balance sheet provides a company with a wide variety of options to navigate any problems, expected or unexpected. Having access to money provides more options to solve problems.
  2. They have a simple and repeatable business. The more complicated the business plan, the more things can go wrong. This is especially true in hard economic times.
  3. Their business isn't sensitive to economic cycles. Cyclical companies can be great investments, but they have natural ebbs and flows where business is really good, and cash is flowing, and downcycles where business is slow and cash flow is low. In a cyclical business, the cycle isn't caused by decisions that management is making. They are simply natural. A business that has minimal exposure to economic cycles won't be outstanding during the upcycle, but also won't go through the downswing. Instead, they are slow, steady, and predictable.

When you don't know whether we will see a recession, whether inflation will spike, whether war will expand or end, or other factors that will drive the market, it is good to have a selection of investments that fit this profile.

Realty Income Corporation (O) is one such investment. Founded in 1969 as "The Monthly Dividend Company," O has paid 669 consecutive monthly dividends. It has increased its dividend for 114 consecutive quarters (28.5 years), and since its listing as a public company, has grown its dividend at a CAGR of 4.2%: Source

O has been a low-volatility investment, with a beta since inception of just 0.49. Through periods of market stress, O's drawdowns have been lower than the S&P 500 as measured by the Vanguard 500 Index Investor, with the exception of COVID.

It's somewhat noteworthy that during the GFC (Subprime Crisis from the above table), which impacted real estate head-on, O sold off less than the S&P 500.

Let's go through our checklist.

  • Does O have a solid balance sheet? Source

O is one of only a handful of REITs with an A- or better rating. Their debt is well-laddered, 99.9% unsecured, and leverage is low for the sector.

  • Does O have a simple and repeatable business?

O buys properties, leases them to tenants using a "triple-net lease", which means the tenants are responsible for most of the property-level expenses. Using this strategy, O has grown its portfolio to over 15,500 properties. It is repeatable and scalable.

  • How sensitive is O's business to economic cycles?

O's operational return, defined as AFFO/share growth + dividends, is typically between 8%-12%, and has never been negative:

For investors, O has provided consistent and growing dividends for decades. It isn't the type of investment that takes off and goes to the moon. It's the type of reliable investment that you can have a lot of confidence in, which will pay you every month and will pay you more next quarter.

In periods where there is a lot of fear, uncertainty, and doubt, that stability is worth its weight in gold.

Conclusion

AGNC and O are two investments I'm getting greedy with while the market is fearful. Both are companies with business models that are relatively low risk. AGNC holds assets that correlate with US Treasuries, which are selling off during the period when the market is in "sell everything" mode, but are also assets that tend to rebound strongly when the economy weakens. The current dip in MBS prices is a great opportunity for AGNC to continue bringing its average asset yield higher.

O is a company that has been through the cycle before. It's seen recessions, wars, instability, credit crises, and more. Through it all, O has paid a monthly dividend with frequent raises. O's fortress balance sheet and scalable business strategy have created a reliable and stable investment option for income investors.

When the market gets fearful, these are two companies I am buying.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: SPY At A Key Fork In The Road
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Like Tom Hanks’ character at the end of the movie Cast Away, the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) is at a key fork in the road. Whether it takes the bullish or bearish path is critical.

The stock market is closed for the Good Friday holiday, so it’s a good time to reflect on the bigger picture. My MoneyShow Chart of the Day shows SPY over a nine-month time frame, along with a trio of exponential moving averages. I also included MACD and RSI indicators.

(Source: StockCharts)

You can see the recent breakdown under the 200-day EMA on high volume. It was followed by a high-volume reversal in the $630s... and a test of the underside of the 200-day. The shorter-term 20-day EMA also broke through the 200-day, while the 50-day EMA has started to roll over.

Another thing worth noting: RSI was steadily declining for some time, even as price kept climbing through early 2026. That “fits” with the broader narrative in place before the Middle East war broke out - namely, that tech and AI stocks were losing steam, investors were rotating into new leaders, and volatility was on the rise in all kinds of places, including metals.

The bulls will argue this is like the Liberation Day selloff in 2025. If President Trump backs down and some kind of Iran deal gets worked out, we’ll be off to the races again.

The bears will counter that markets have been weakening behind the scenes for a while. Not to mention that last year’s tariff threats weren’t accompanied by $100+ oil and $4+ gasoline.

All I can say with certainty is that we’re at a critical market juncture. If SPY takes a “wrong” turn, it might be time to insure your portfolio against grimmer outcomes.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: Seritage Growth Properties: Limited Recent Asset Sale Progress (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Seritage Growth Properties (SRG)(SRG.PR.A) reported Q4 2025 results that showed relatively slow asset sale progress. Seritage closed on the sales of the three assets that it had under contract with no due diligence contingencies, but had only minor progress to report other than that.

It had previously mentioned (in mid-November 2025) having other negotiations and contracts (with subjects) that could result in $118 million in additional gross proceeds, but currently only has 2026 contracts that are expected to $17 million in gross proceeds (including a sale that closed in Q1 2026).

That limited sales progress over the last 4.5 months increases the chances of Seritage either receiving relatively weak prices for its remaining assets and/or taking longer to sell its assets than I previously expected. Thus I am reducing my estimate of Seritage's value from $4.00 per share to $3.25 per share and note that there is also higher uncertainly around that estimated value now.

Slowing Asset Sale Progress

In mid-November 2025, Seritage reported that it had three assets under contract with no due diligence contingencies for combined anticipated gross proceeds of $170 million. These sales closed in Q4 2025.

Seritage also mentioned at that time that it had one asset under contract for $70.8 million, subject to a long dated closing and the pursuit of a master plan amendment. It also mentioned that it was negotiating definitive purchase and sale agreements on three joint venture assets that would result in combined anticipated gross distributions of around $47.3 million.

Seritage indicated that it received a distribution of $5.7 million in Q1 2026 from an unconsolidated entity due to the sale of a portion of the underlying property. It also noted that at the end of Q1 2026, it had one joint venture asset under contract for anticipated proceeds of $11 million.

Seritage didn't mention that the $70.8 million asset is still under contract, which may indicate that that deal has stalled out for now. It had previously mentioned the status of that asset in multiple earnings reports.

As well, the progress on the sale of two of the joint venture assets (that were being negotiated) is uncertain.

Cash Position

Seritage ended up with $62 million in cash (including restricted cash) at the end of 2025. This was in-line with my expectation that it would finish 2025 with approximately $60 million in cash.

Seritage reported that it had $59 million in cash (including restricted cash) at the end of Q1 2026, so its cash burn (excluding proceeds from asset sales) was around $9 million during the quarter. This appears to be in-line with my expectation that Seritage's cash burn would be under $10 million per quarter after its Q4 2025 asset sales were completed.

Seritage has $2.1 million per quarter in preferred dividends and interest costs (with a $50 million term loan balance). Seritage's revenues should generally offset its property operating expenses and real estate taxes. Seritage reported $4.2 million in Q4 2025 revenues and $4.4 million in Q4 2025 property operating expenses and real estate taxes.

Thus it may have around $7 million per quarter in combined G&A and real estate development capex now. This combined amount was approximately $10 million in Q4 2025, but it completed the sale of several assets during the quarter, reducing its development capex needs.

Seritage's year-end filing included a going concern note since its "sales of assets under contract with closings that are deemed probable" and cash on hand was not enough to fund both its ongoing cash burn and its $50 million July 2026 term loan maturity.

Seritage indicated that it looking at various financing measures to address that term loan maturity. I don't believe this will be a major problem given the relatively small amount remaining on the term loan. There is a risk that the financing measures could increase Seritage's costs a bit though.

2025 Real Estate Sale Proceeds

Seritage received approximately $218 million in net proceeds from real estate sales in 2025, compared to gross proceeds of approximately $231 million.

It reported a $20 million gain on the sale of real estate in 2025, but also reported $20 million in combined impairment of real estate assets (due to a sale of an asset at below book value) and losses from the sale of interests in unconsolidated entities.

Net proceeds were around 94% to 95% of gross proceeds for the 2025 transactions. Gross proceeds appear to have tracked closely to balance sheet values.

Notes On Valuation

The remaining balance sheet value of Seritage's real estate assets was around $311 million at the end of 2025. I've adjusted this to $320 million after estimating future property development spending and depreciation.

If Seritage nets $290 million (around 91% of balance sheet value) for those remaining assets, it would have a value of approximately $3.25 per share in the end. This is based on $62 million in cash at the end of 2025, $50 million in additional operational cash burn before it winds up operations and $120 million in term loan debt and preferred shares that it needs to repay.

This is a $0.75 per share reduction compared to my prior estimate of Seritage's value. Seritage's ongoing costs appear to be in-line with my prior expectations. However, the lack of further asset sale progress is a concern and increases the odds that Seritage will accept lower prices for its remaining assets and/or take longer to sell those assets. The reduction in Seritage's estimated share price reflects those items.

Seritage also mentioned the possibility of a strategic transaction (now that it has a simplified portfolio).

Conclusion

Seritage's Q4 2025 earnings report indicated that it closed on the sale of three assets that it had under contract with no due diligence contingencies. However, beyond that its asset sale progress was pretty limited. It reported a $5.7 million distribution in Q1 2026 from the sale of a portion of a property and has one other asset under contract for anticipated proceeds of $11 million.

Due to the apparent slow progress with asset sales, I am reducing Seritage's estimated common share value from $4.00 per share to $3.25 per share. I still generally believe (in my base case scenario) that there is some upside to Seritage at its current share price. However, due to the increased uncertainty and the relatively limited margin of safety (another 10% reduction in real estate asset value would drop its estimated value below $2.75), I consider myself neutral on Seritage now.
